title: practical english usage - Part 209
content:
268 preparatory         it:  subɪeet     ________________ grammar •  269  preparatory it: object I found it strange being in her house.

This structure is also possible with -ing form objects.

2 / found       it  strange         being      ...

I take it that you won't be working tomorrow.

Note also the idiom I take it that. .. (= I assume that...).

I love it when you sing. I hate it when strangers use my first name.

But note the structure I like/love/hate it when . ..

I remember that we were very happy, (     I remember it that-...) not I cannot bear to see people crying, (     I cannot bear-it-tesee-people crying) complement after the verb.

Note that this structure is not normally used when there is no adjective or noun The Prime Minister has made clear that he will not allow a free vote.

It is quite often dropped from make (it) clear that...

George made it clear what he wanted.

I thought it strange that she hadn't written.

We found it tiring to listen to him. My blister made it a problem to walk.

subject + verb + it + complement + infinitive/clause we  prefer 7 find it difficult to talk to you'.

noun complement. For example, instead of saying 7 find to talk to you difficult', object of a verb is an infinitive or a clause, and when this has an adjective or We   can sometimes use it as a ‘ preparatory object'. This happens when the 1  I find     it  difficult         to   talk     to   you.

269 preparatory         it:  object expenses... is the complement of the impression.) irL (that travel but not The impression was given that travel expenses would be paid,         It was (to sit on is the complement of comfortable.) but not This chair is comfortable to sit on.         #  is-eemfortable-te siT&n-this chai* not as a preparatory complement.

It can be used as a preparatory subject, or as a preparatory object (►  269), but 7 preparatory         it  not   possible      for   complements For passive structures with it as a preparatory subject, ►    63.

It's strange the way you know what I'm thinking.

It's amazing the way (that) they work together.

This is very common with the way .. .

It's wonderful the enthusiasm that the children show.

relative clause.

But in an informal style, it can be a preparatory subject for noun + not The new concert hall is wonderful, (     It-s wonderful-the-new concert-halL) It is not normally used as a preparatory subject for noun phrases.

6 It's  amazing         the    way     they     work      together.

Information StructureSection 25 grammar •  270  inversion: auxiliary verb before subject For more details of these structures, ►    309.

‘ I'm hungry.' ‘ So am.' 'I don't like opera.' ‘ Neither/Nor do I.' verb + subject.

In 'short answers’  and similar structures, these words are followed by auxiliary 4 after    so,  neither,        nor May all your wishes come true!

May can come  before the subject in wishes.

How beautiful are the flowers! What a peaceful place is Skegness!

exclamations after how and what.

In a rather old-fashioned literary style, inversion is sometimes found in Have you got a surprise coming! Was I mad!

(non-negative) questions (but with different intonation).

In spoken American English, exclamations often have the same form as ordinary Isn't it cold? Hasn't she got lovely eyes?

Exclamations (►  223) often have the form of negative questions (►    218).

I wondered how reliable was the information I had been given.

questions after how, especially when the subject is long.

However, in formal writing inversion is sometimes used with be in indirect the film starting.) not I wondered what time the film was starting. (     .. .what time was Indirect questions do not usually have this order (►  260).

You're coming tomorrow?

Spoken questions do not always have this word order (►   302).

Where the concert is taking place?) Where is the concert taking place? (NOT   W  here is taking place the concert?

Have your father and mother arrived? (     H ave arrived your father and 1  questions subject of a clause in several different structures.
